_meta.notes: Ch2 L8. Mahal, kita, mahal kita.

cards.body: Mahal is one of the most interesting words in Tagalog. It means two completely different things.

example.note: Ko = my/I. Si = name marker. You already know both.

cards.body: Mahal also means 'expensive.' Context tells you which one.

example.note: 'Mahal ang kape' = the coffee is expensive. 'Mahal kita' = I love you. Totally different.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: The double meaning isn't a coincidence. The connection is: what is precious and valued is both loved and costly.

cards.explanation: Mahal kita = I love you. Kita = I (to) you.

cards.body: Kita is a special pronoun. It means 'I to you' in one word. Only used with mahal.

example.note: Kita = I love YOU specifically. One word, two people.

cards.body: Filipinos don't say 'mahal kita' casually. It carries weight. But parents, grandparents, and partners say it sincerely.

cards.source_text: 'Mahal ang sapatos!' your friend says at the mall.
cards.explanation: Mahal + ang [thing] = expensive. Context: shopping = expensive, not love.

cards.prompt: Tell your nanay you love her.
cards.explanation: Mahal ko si Nanay = I love Mom. Ko = I/my, si = name marker.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: Filipinos more commonly show love through actions than words. Cooking for someone, giving food, asking 'Kumain ka na ba?' are all expressions of mahal.
'Mahal na mahal kita' = I love you very much. Doubling the mahal intensifies it.
Kita is one of the most compact pronouns in any language. 'I love you' in three syllables: ma-hal ki-ta.
